items.q: Which energy system fuels a marathon?
items.why: Students associate hard exercise with anaerobic systems. Long-duration steady-state work uses AEROBIC metabolism (oxygen + carbs/fats) - efficient but slower to ramp up. Anaerobic systems dominate brief intense efforts.

items.q: What is the rough formula for maximum heart rate?
items.why: Students misremember the formula. 220 - age is the classic estimate (rough; varies individually). A 20-year-old's estimated max HR is around 200 bpm; an 80-year-old's is around 140.

Students lump all training together. Plyometrics specifically targets POWER - explosive jumps and bounds use rapid eccentric-then-concentric muscle action, training the stretch-shortening cycle for fast force production.

items.q: HIIT typically involves work bouts at:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: ≥85% HRmax. “~50% HRmax” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Running speed at VO₂max. “Sprint top speed” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If a runner has VO₂max = 70 mL kg⁻¹ min⁻¹ and a 4-min interval at 100% VO₂max repeated 4 times, total at-max minutes is?
items.why: Exact answer: 16. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
